original_abstract:
This book critically examines the dynamics of power within global health and the consequences these forces exert on real-world outcomes. In *Rethinking Global Health*, the author raises the fundamental questions: “Who is global health truly for?” and “What barriers prevent us from building responses that meet people where they are?” Spanning a wide array of topics—from global mental health initiatives to the frontline response to Ebola—the text weaves together rigorous power analyses with personal reflections and interviews gleaned from the author’s decade-long career in the field. 

The narrative interrogates why the pursuit of universal solutions frequently drifts from its intended path. To aid this inquiry, the book introduces readers to various analytical frameworks, including a specialized adaptation of the ‘matrix of domination’ tailored for health practice. Through this critique, Dr. Burgess proposes a pioneering model of "Transformative Global Health." This framework challenges researchers and practitioners to adopt new orienting principles that center community interests and local voices in every stage of planning and implementation. Essential for students and academics in public health, this book is equally vital for activists, practitioners, and anyone committed to the global pursuit of health equity.
humanized_abstract:
This book uncovers power relations in the global health field and their effects. Rethinking Global Health: Bold Solutions for A Troubled World, by Gerald T. Keusch, Editor in Chief, Global Health: Scientific and Practical Research (GHSPR) Corresponding Editor, International Journal of Infectious Diseases asks for the first time some fundamental questions and answers in a world where an unequal system dominates and obstructs intervention on the ground aimed at improving lives. Questions such as: “Who is global health truly for?” or “What makes it so hard to create meaningful programs that address the needs of communities as they change over time?” Along the way the book examines a variety of examples from the world of global health: psychiatry, Zika, HIV prevention, vaccination, harm reduction for drug users, and the response to Ebola among others. Based on the author’s ten years of practical experience in the global health arena including on the frontlines of the battle against the Ebola outbreak in West Africa, the book offers original insights, using power analysis as a vehicle to combine social science and firsthand accounts.

This book critically explores the why the universal quest for global best practice health interventions so often goes off the rails. To aid this exercise, the book draws on a range of theoretical tools and perspectives, including a specially designed 'matrix of domination' framework for health practices. In this critique, Dr. Burgess proposes a groundbreaking new paradigm of "Transformative Global Health"; one that requires a fundamental shift in how global health researchers and practitioners "orient" themselves to health problems and their solutions. This book is an essential resource for students and scholars of public health, as well as for activists, practitioners and advocates interested in advancing the goal of global health equity.
